text: The surgeon positions the trocar in the upper abdomen to gain a clear view of the large uterus during a total laparoscopic hysterectomy. The round ligament is cut, followed by the left fallopian tube. The bipolar device is used to coagulate the utero-ovarian ligament and control the left adnexal vein. After coagulation, the lateral attachments of the enlarged uterus are cut, maintaining a bloodless field.

The surgeon increases uterine mobility by cutting the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, taking care to avoid the deep uterine vein. The round ligament and right tube are cut using the harmonic, while the right utero-ovarian ligament is controlled with the bipolar before being cut. The dissection continues toward the upper part of the right uterosacral ligament, maintaining a bloodless field.

The surgeon performs vesicovaginal dissection by cutting the peritoneum between the bladder and the isthmus. The Halban fascia is identified and cut, allowing the bladder to be pushed downward, revealing the anterior vaginal wall. The upper part of the right bladder pillar is cut, maintaining a bloodless field using either harmonic or classical devices such as bipolar and scissors.

The surgeon skeletonizes the left uterine pedicle, applying bipolar coagulation to the artery and vein before safely cutting them. The posterior cervical vagina and vessel are dissected, followed by similar actions on the anterior medial part of the left uterine pedicle. A ten millimeter forceps is used to visualize the right uterine pedicle, where the artery is coagulated and cut with the harmonic ace, followed by the vein, resulting in a bloodless procedure.

The surgeon coagulates the uterine pedicle using bipolar forceps and cuts it with the Harmonic Ace, ensuring a bloodless procedure. The posterior leaf of the broad ligament is dissected toward the upper part of the uterosacral ligament. The posterior aspect of the vagina is progressively opened and safely cut, including the right cervical vaginal vessel. The surgeon prepares for an anterior colpotomy, preserving the left uterine vein for a classical intrafascial hysterectomy.

The surgeon uses a monopolar needle to cut the anterior and right lateral aspects of the vagina, while the posterior vaginal valve is visible, having been cut initially. The procedure remains bloodless despite the large size of the uterus. An intra-abdominal morcellation is performed using a chardonnay knife, an endoscopic blade, inserted through a 10mm trocar positioned between the umbilicus and pubic line. The uterus is cut with the cold knife and extracted using laparoscopic forceps, minimizing vaginal trauma.

The surgeon completes the uterine extraction and proceeds with vaginal closure using a Vicryl suture and a fourteen millimeter needle. The closure begins at the lateral aspect, with a posterior stitch placed in the upper part of the uterosacral ligament to reduce the risk of postoperative vaginal prolapse. The stitch encompasses the entire mucosa and peritoneum to minimize postoperative bleeding.

The surgeon performs the vaginal closure using an extract of oil knotting technique, creating a rudder knot that is fast, quick, and safe. Interrupted sutures are preferred over running ones for better cicatrization. The vaginal edges are closed without using bipolar coagulation, and intracorporeal knotting technique is also utilized.
